does: This paper identifies and addresses a training-inference mismatch in text-to-image diffusion models where initial noise distributions differ between training and inference phases. The authors propose a noise projector—a lightweight additional module that applies text-conditioned refinement to initial noise before denoising, mapping prompt-agnostic Gaussian noise to prompt-aware noise that better matches the distribution seen during Stable Diffusion training. The method operates without modifying the pretrained diffusion model itself, instead using vision-language model feedback on generated images to guide noise refinement and improve prompt alignment.
connects: This work builds on the extensive literature studying text-to-image misalignment in diffusion models, extending beyond existing approaches that either modify denoising dynamics or rely on multiple samples with post-selection. The paper connects to prior research identifying distributional gaps between training and inference in generative models, a well-known source of artifacts in other domains. It also relates to work on conditioning mechanisms in diffusion models and the role of latent space properties in generation quality. The use of vision-language models for feedback signals builds on recent trends of leveraging multimodal models for generation quality assessment.
enables: This approach opens new directions for improving diffusion model reliability through lightweight, model-agnostic interventions. The noise projection framework could be extended to other conditional generation tasks beyond text-to-image synthesis, and the explicit modeling of prompt-aware noise distributions may inform theoretical understanding of why certain prompts generate misaligned outputs. The method suggests future work on characterizing and correcting other distribution mismatches in generative models, potentially enabling more robust control over generation quality without expensive retraining. This could also motivate research into directly training diffusion models with prompt-conditioned noise priors to eliminate the gap at the source.
